question_title: What will be the value of "10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate" on 2026-03-12?
summary: The 10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate (T10YIE) was 2.25% on Feb 27, the most recent observation. The series has been in a downward trend from ~2.36 in late January, entering a stable regime since Feb 12 with mean 2.274% and std 0.014%. Resolution date is March 12, 2026 — 9 trading days away. A key catalyst is the February CPI release on March 11 (one day before resolution), which adds significant event volatility. Falling 10Y nominal yields (from 4.28 to 3.96 in Feb) reflect risk-off sentiment that has pressured breakevens lower. Tariff pass-through risks create some upside potential for inflation expectations. Monte Carlo simulation using empirical volatility (0.015/day normal, 0.030 on CPI day) with 300k paths produces a distribution centered near 2.247 with P10=2.18 and P90=2.31.

factors.description: Current value is 2.25% (Feb 27), anchoring the distribution center near this level
Stable regime since Feb 12 with mean 2.274%, std 0.014% - suggests mild mean reversion upward from 2.25
Mild downward drift over full 6-month sample (-0.0013/day) persists, last reading was a new regime low
CPI release on March 11 (one day before resolution) adds significant event volatility ~0.03 historically
Tariff pass-through to consumers increasing in 2026 per multiple sources - upside risk to inflation expectations
10Y nominal yield falling sharply (4.28 to 3.96 in Feb) indicating risk-off sentiment, depressing breakevens

reasoning:
**Forecast: 10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate on 2026-03-12**

**Distribution (percentiles):**
| Percentile | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| P10 | 2.18% |
| P20 | 2.20% |
| P40 | 2.23% |
| P60 | 2.26% |
| P80 | 2.29% |
| P90 | 2.31% |

**Key reasoning:**
- **Current level**: 2.25% (Feb 27), in a stable regime since Feb 12 oscillating around 2.27%
- **Downward trend**: Fell from 2.36% in late January, driven by falling nominal yields (10Y from 4.28% to 3.96%)
- **CPI event risk**: February CPI releases March 11 (day before resolution), adding ~2x normal daily volatility. This widens the distribution meaningfully
- **Tariff upside risk**: Increasing pass-through of tariff costs to consumers could push inflation expectations higher
- **Monte Carlo basis**: 300k simulations using empirical daily volatility (0.015 normal, 0.030 on CPI day) center the distribution at ~2.247% with symmetric tails
sources_consulted: [10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate (T10YIEM) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/T10YIEM)
